messages.content:
请帮我写一段 benchmark 的引言部分，主要是翻译一下代码块中的中文，引用的部分功能供你参考
  
## 第一段
> 2-3 句话介绍论文的背景，需要让读者接受到的信息包括
> 
> 1.  Task 所处的大背景是什么（时代/历史的洪流）
>     
> 2.  你做的是一个什么样的 task/这个task 有什么样的价值
>     
```Markdown
llm 的蓬勃发展使得越来越多的人利用其来协助完成生活和工作的事宜，尤其是写作这一领域；作为写作助手，如何衡量llm 在用户意图预测上的能力，即无缝生成用户所想，是一件不可缺少的事情。
```
## 第二段
> 3-4句话讲清楚这个 task 所处的状态/难点，以及过去方法的局限性
> 
> 1.  这个 task 目前处在啥状态，有啥难点/挑战（这个难点/挑战）往往和本论文提出的方法有关
>     
> 2.  过去的工作大概分为那几种，总结归纳若干脉络，以及点破现在存在的（本文要解决的问题）
>     
> 3.  讲清楚本文要解决的这个问题的价值
>     
```Markdown
现有bench注重长文本写作，多数以llm-as-judger，针对不同 domain的写作文章自主动态调整维度系数，但是不稳定、主观性较大，缺少互动交互环节；一些 Arena 相关的工作从真实性、完整性等方便比较模型生成的内容与参考内容的优胜比例，但与衡量用户原始意图有一定偏差；部分人机协作的评价工作以编程和工具调用为主，少数写作领域的 bench 事先定义粗粒度的“意图标签”，与真实写作场景有偏差；
```
## 第三段
> 3-4句话讲清楚我们干了啥，highlight 核心motivation 和 contribution
> 
> 第3、4段的逻辑、篇幅和段落数会相对自由，核心是能够讲清楚核心 motivation 和 contribution，以及优势
```Markdown
为了弥补这个缺陷，我们提出了 IntentionBench，一个综合的 benchmark来评估写作助手对于真实专家写作的意图预测；其次，我们构建有状态以及无状态的评测框架，single-turn 的方式基于前文预测后续内容，以及 multi-turn 动态交互式的预测完一整篇文章，模拟真实的写作环节；为了衡量模型对意图的准确性，我们构建了基于 Checklist 的Acceptance Verifier、基于实体难度的编辑距离评估，以及rubric-based 的 placeholder，以全面准确评价。同时，我们的 bench 中为写作助手提供文章画像工具、用户画像工具， 并设计了多智能体的 pipeline用于测试；测试的条目来自真实高质量的人类专家写作文章， 2273 个query 中涵盖自然科学、人文、数码科技等 15 种场景。
```

<details type="reasoning" done="true" duration="18">
<summary>Thought for 18 seconds</summary>
> 用户需要我帮忙翻译和润色一段benchmark论文的引言部分。让我按照三段的结构来翻译和改进：
> 
> 第一段：介绍背景和任务价值
> 第二段：说明现有方法的局限性
> 第三段：介绍我们的贡献
> 
> 我需要保持学术论文的专业性和流畅性，同时确保逻辑清晰。
> 
> 第一段翻译：
> 原文："llm 的蓬勃发展使得越来越多的人利用其来协助完成生活和工作的事宜，尤其是写作这一领域；作为写作助手，如何衡量llm 在用户意图预测上的能力，即无缝生成用户所想，是一件不可缺少的事情。"
> 
> 改进版本：
> The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has led to their widespread adoption as assistants in various daily and professional tasks, particularly in the domain of writing. As writing assistants, it is crucial to measure LLMs' capability in predicting user intentions—that is, their ability to seamlessly generate content that aligns with what users have in mind.
> 
> 第二段翻译：
> 原文提到了现有benchmark的几个问题：
> 1. 注重长文本写作，使用LLM-as-judge，但不稳定、主观性大
> 2. Arena工作缺少互动环节
> 3. 人机协作主要在编程领域
> 4. 写作领域的bench使用粗粒度标签，与真实场景有偏差
> 
> 改进版本：
> Current benchmarks primarily focus on long-form writing and often employ LLM-as-judge approaches that dynamically adjust evaluation criteria across different domains, yet suffer from instability and high subjectivity, while lacking interactive evaluation components. 
> 
> Arena-based works compare model-generated content against reference content in terms of authenticity and completeness, but this approach deviates from measuring users' original intentions. Although some human-AI collaboration evaluation efforts exist, they predominantly concentrate on programming and tool invocation tasks. The few writing-oriented benchmarks that do exist rely on predefined coarse-grained "intention labels," which fail to capture the nuances of real-world writing scenarios.
> 
> 我们提出了一个创新的评测框架IntentionBench，旨在精确捕捉用户写作意图的细微差别。通过设计有状态和无状态的评估方法，我们的方法能够更全面地评估大语言模型在写作辅助中的性能，填补了现有基准测试中的关键空白。
> 
> 我们的方法不仅包括单轮和多轮交互式预测，还开发了多维度评估系统，包括清单验证、实体难度加权编辑距离和基于规范的评估。这个基准测试覆盖了从自然科学到人文领域的15个不同场景，共包含2,273个查询，全部源自高质量的专家写作文章。
> 
> 通过提供文档和用户画像工具，以及多智能体流水线，我们为写作助手提供了全面的意图预测评估平台。
</details>
# Introduction

The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has led to their widespread adoption as assistants in various daily and professional tasks, particularly in the domain of writing. As writing assistants, it is crucial to measure LLMs' capability in predicting user intentions—that is, their ability to seamlessly generate content that aligns with what users have in mind.

Current benchmarks primarily focus on long-form writing and often employ LLM-as-judge approaches that dynamically adjust evaluation criteria across different domains, yet suffer from instability and high subjectivity, while lacking interactive evaluation components. Arena-based works compare model-generated content against reference content in terms of authenticity and completeness, but this approach deviates from measuring users' original intentions. Although some human-AI collaboration evaluation efforts exist, they predominantly concentrate on programming and tool invocation tasks. The few writing-oriented benchmarks that do exist rely on predefined coarse-grained "intention labels," which fail to capture the nuances of real-world writing scenarios.

To address these limitations, we introduce **IntentionBench**, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating writing assistants' ability to predict intentions in authentic expert writing. We construct both stateful and stateless evaluation frameworks: a single-turn approach that predicts subsequent content based on preceding context, and a multi-turn interactive approach that dynamically predicts content throughout an entire article, simulating real writing scenarios. To accurately measure models' intention prediction capability, we develop a multi-faceted evaluation system consisting of a Checklist-based Acceptance Verifier, an entity-difficulty-weighted edit distance metric, and rubric-based assessments. Additionally, our benchmark provides writing assistants with document profiling and user profiling tools, and incorporates a multi-agent pipeline for testing. The evaluation queries are derived from high-quality, authentic expert-written articles, encompassing 2,273 queries across 15 diverse scenarios including natural sciences, humanities, and digital technology.
不要简单的翻译，可以适当调整优化，以及润色一下语言表述

> 1. 保持学术写作的专业性和流畅性
> 2. 调整句子结构，使其更加地道和连贯
> 3. 增强逻辑性和说服力
> 4. 使用更精准的学术表达
> 
> 让我重新组织这三段内容：
> 
> 第一段：背景和任务价值
> - LLM的发展趋势
> - 作为写作助手的重要性
> - 意图预测能力的必要性
> 
> 第二段：现有问题和挑战
> - 现有benchmark的局限性
> - 分几类说明问题
> - 强调评估gap
> 
> 第三段：我们的方案
> - 提出IntentionBench
> - 核心特点和创新
> - 数据规模和覆盖范围
> 
> 我需要让表达更加学术化、流畅，避免直译的生硬感。
</details>
# Introduction

The proliferation of large language models (LLMs) has catalyzed their integration into everyday workflows, with writing assistance emerging as one of the most prominent applications. A fundamental challenge in developing effective writing assistants lies in their ability to anticipate user intentions—generating content that naturally aligns with what users envision, before they explicitly articulate it.

Despite growing interest in evaluating writing assistance capabilities, existing benchmarks exhibit several critical limitations. First, while many benchmarks focus on long-form content generation, they predominantly rely on LLM-as-judge frameworks that dynamically weight evaluation dimensions across domains, introducing instability and subjectivity into assessments. Second, arena-style evaluations compare model outputs against reference texts using metrics like fidelity and completeness, but these surface-level comparisons fail to capture whether models truly understand underlying user intentions. Third, although human-AI collaboration has been explored in programming and tool-use contexts, writing-oriented benchmarks remain scarce. The few that exist typically employ predefined, coarse-grained intention categories that poorly reflect the nuanced, context-dependent nature of real-world writing.

To bridge this gap, we present **IntentionBench**, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate how well writing assistants predict intentions in authentic expert writing. Our framework features both stateless and stateful evaluation paradigms: single-turn prediction tasks that assess next-content generation given prior context, and multi-turn interactive sessions that simulate iterative writing workflows where models progressively complete entire articles. We introduce a multi-dimensional evaluation suite comprising (1) a Checklist-based Acceptance Verifier for precision assessment, (2) an entity-difficulty-weighted edit distance metric that accounts for varying prediction challenges, and (3) rubric-based holistic evaluations. To support realistic testing scenarios, IntentionBench provides document and user profiling tools, and implements a multi-agent evaluation pipeline. Our benchmark comprises 2,273 queries extracted from high-quality, human-authored articles spanning 15 diverse domains—including natural sciences, humanities, and digital technology—ensuring broad coverage of real-world writing scenarios.

“ We introduce a multi-dimensional evaluation suite comprising (1) a Checklist-based Acceptance Verifier for precision assessment, (2) an entity-difficulty-weighted edit distance metric that accounts for varying prediction challenges, and (3) rubric-based holistic evaluations. “
  
这段话再优化一下，涵盖如下信息：
基于 Checklist 的Acceptance Verifier用于判断是否预测出意图，基于实体难度的编辑距离来评估意图预测的完成度，以及rubric-based 的 placeholder来评估对于高信息密度任务的完成情况

> 用户希望我优化这段话，并且要涵盖更具体的信息：
> 1. Checklist-based Acceptance Verifier - 用于判断是否预测出意图
> 2. 基于实体难度的编辑距离 - 评估意图预测的完成度
> 3. rubric-based placeholder - 评估高信息密度任务的完成情况
> 
> 我需要让这段话更清晰地表达这三个评估方法的具体用途，同时保持学术写作的流畅性。
> 
> 可以这样组织：
> - 首先说明我们引入了多维度评估套件
> - 然后分别说明三个组件：
>   1. Checklist-based verifier判断意图是否被预测到
>   2. Entity-difficulty-weighted edit distance衡量预测的完成程度
>   3. Rubric-based评估处理高信息密度内容的能力
> 
> 让我写几个版本：
> 
> 版本1：
> We introduce a multi-dimensional evaluation suite comprising (1) a Checklist-based Acceptance Verifier that determines whether target intentions are successfully predicted, (2) an entity-difficulty-weighted edit distance metric that measures the completeness of intention prediction, and (3) rubric-based holistic assessments that evaluate performance on high-information-density writing tasks.
> 
> 版本2：
> To accurately assess intention prediction, we develop a three-pronged evaluation framework: (1) a Checklist-based Acceptance Verifier that validates whether models capture target intentions, (2) an entity-difficulty-weighted edit distance metric that quantifies the degree of intention fulfillment, and (3) rubric-based evaluations that measure models' handling of high-information-density content.
> 
> 版本3：
> We design a comprehensive evaluation framework with three complementary components: (1) a Checklist-based Acceptance Verifier to verify whether intended content is predicted, (2) an entity-difficulty-weighted edit distance metric to quantify the completeness of intention realization, and (3) rubric-based assessments to evaluate performance on information-dense writing tasks.
>  
> I'll refine the approach to enhance academic precision and clarity, focusing on the nuanced evaluation methods that provide comprehensive insight into intention prediction performance.
</details>
Here's an optimized version:

We design a comprehensive evaluation framework with three complementary components: (1) a Checklist-based Acceptance Verifier that determines whether models successfully predict target intentions, (2) an entity-difficulty-weighted edit distance metric that quantifies the completeness of intention fulfillment, and (3) rubric-based assessments that evaluate models' performance on information-dense writing tasks.

Or alternatively:

To rigorously assess intention prediction capabilities, we develop a multi-faceted evaluation suite: (1) a Checklist-based Acceptance Verifier for verifying whether target intentions are captured, (2) an entity-difficulty-weighted edit distance metric for measuring the degree of intention realization, and (3) rubric-based holistic evaluations for assessing performance on high-information-density content generation.
